item.title: Shell
item.description: A gateway to controlling the system

item.title: AWK
item.description: A language designed for text processing.

item.title: B
item.description: An early programming language used in the early Multics project.

item.title: Backpressure
item.description: Tell producers to stop sending data because the consumer cannot keep up

item.title: cat
item.description: A standard Unix utility that reads files sequentially, writing them to standard output.

item.title: Daemons
item.description: Service processes that run in the background and supervise the system or provide functionality to other processes

item.title: fork
item.description: An operation whereby a process creates a copy of itself

item.title: Legendary figures
item.description: Legendary figures in Unix history

item.title: man
item.description: Command to get detailed documentation of different unix components, including other cmds.

item.title: Memory leaks
item.description: Improper managing of memory by a computer program

item.title: null
item.description: The null device is a device file that discards all data written to it but reports that the write operation succeeded.

item.title: Oregano
item.description: The Oregano incident

item.title: Pipes
item.description: Connect cmd outputs to cmd inputs to create complex pipelines

item.title: Shell script
item.description: An operation whereby a process creates a copy of itself

item.title: su
item.description: Command to switch your identity

item.title: tar
item.description: A software utility for collecting multiple files into a single archive file.

item.title: The c programming language
item.description: A key programming language in the creation of Unix

item.title: troff
item.description: A program in formatting documents for the document processing system.

item.title: usr
item.description: A directory holding user home directories.

item.title: uucp
item.description: A Unix program for file copy requests.
